reasons: Baxia directly reduces Ruby's healing value, making her extended fight pattern much weaker.
Ruby depends heavily on sustain from repeated skill hits, so anti-heal pressure is a direct answer.

proof.summary: Ruby wants to heal through repeated skill hits, while Baxia reduces healing and shield effects during the fight.
worksBestWhen: Baxia applies anti-sustain before Ruby lands multiple AoE skills.
The team focuses Ruby while her healing is reduced.
failureCases: Ruby avoids Baxia and hits multiple squishy targets.
Baxia cannot stay close enough to apply pressure.

reasons: Valir can push Ruby away before she chains area crowd control.
Ruby becomes less effective if she cannot stay close enough to repeatedly hit skills and heal.

proof.summary: Ruby wants close-range control and sustain, while Valir can deny melee uptime with knockback and slow.
worksBestWhen: Valir saves knockback for Ruby's engage.
Valir positions outside Ruby's pull range.
failureCases: Ruby flanks or Flicker-engages before Valir reacts.
Valir wastes knockback on a frontline bait.

reasons: Karrie can pressure Ruby's durability with repeated true-damage marks.
Ruby struggles if Karrie stays outside her hook and control range.

proof.summary: Ruby wants to outlast enemies, while Karrie can cut through durable sustain fighters from range.
worksBestWhen: Karrie has peel and does not stand inside Ruby's pull range.
Karrie has enough attack speed to stack marks reliably.
failureCases: Ruby catches Karrie with Flicker or teammate setup.

reasons: Dyrroth can punish Ruby before she gets repeated skill casts and lifesteal value.
His defense reduction can make Ruby's short trades riskier.

proof.summary: Ruby wants to stretch fights, while Dyrroth can force a short burst trade before Ruby stabilizes through repeated skill healing.
worksBestWhen: Dyrroth trades early and avoids Ruby's repeated crowd-control chain.
Dyrroth has enhanced skill timing ready.
failureCases: Dyrroth overextends into Ruby's crowd control.
Ruby gets anti-burst items and teammate follow-up.

reasons: Gatotkaca is a better reviewed replacement for Pharsa because his disruption directly interferes with Ruby's close-range fighting pattern.
His taunt and area engage can interrupt Ruby's rhythm and force her to spend time attacking him instead of freely controlling the backline.

proof.summary: Ruby wants to chain close-range control and sustain through skill hits, while Gatotkaca can disrupt the fight with taunt, frontline presence, and area engage.
worksBestWhen: Gatotkaca enters after Ruby commits to a close-range fight.
The team uses the taunt or knock-up window to focus Ruby or protect the backline.
failureCases: Ruby avoids Gatotkaca and pulls a softer target instead.
Gatotkaca engages without teammate damage or anti-heal support.
